high_level_task_goal: Current backtesting platforms aren't adequate to test modern financial indicators built in TypeScript or Rust, such as one called OTT which has been around for a long time, but now is being modified and use for cryptocurrency markets and auto-trading. Sol-Bot is an auto indicator and is part of a larger auto trading idea that that follows the SOL_USD market and sends an alert when a market trend flips (when a market goes from bullish to bearish or vise-versa), but it's not limited to SOL_BTC. It can be used with any pairing such as BTC_USD and even traditional stocks like Apple (AAPL) on the NASDAQ or the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) on the New York Stock Exchange. There's a fast growing need for better, faster technology to backtest automated trading concepts. My research to solve this problem led me to QuantConnect LEAN engine - an open source project that can test indicators built in different languages that other platforms can't. I decided it would be a good exercise to build a backtesting platform that can keep up with and verify new emerging indicators or improved traditional ones regardless of what language they were built in. I'm testing TypeScript indicators because most cryptocurrency exchanges SDKs are TypeScript-based, built in Node. Also, this project has a front-end built with NextJS/React in TypeScript, the main engine is Python based which is what the Quant Connect LEAN Engine is built upon. So this project challenges an AI not only to work in multiple languages, but there is an interesting caveat with the QuantConnect engine which is if it is used locally then it needs to do its backtests with Docker images that it builds and tests in a Docker container. I'll have a Docker daemon running on my MacBook Pro during the testing, and if the models can see it, then they can use QuantConnect. If they cannot, then they'll have to come up with an architecture to build the backtesting engine in pure Python and discuss with me prior to them building it. What I really like about this project is the multiple language, um, requirement, but more so, um, the possibility of having to architect and work with a human developer to r-- uh, achieve the desired effect. This tests a model's ability not only to code, but to brainstorm, architect, and build an engine that fulfills a very specific and complex real world technology need.
initial_prompt: So, today you're going to build a platform which backtests financial candle data against different indicators. Today we're going to backtest one — it's called an OTT indicator. And the data you're going to use is from Binance, and it's in CSV form. You're also going to build a frontend to view the data in Next.js. And you're going to build the backtesting engine in Python. Now my preference is that you build the engine using QuantConnect LEAN Engine which is an open source Apache 2.0 project and here is the GitHub URL — https://github.com/QuantConnect/Lean . Now this is free to use locally with no subscription, but you have to use your own data which we have, and you have to install it locally with pip, but the main issue is that if you use it that way, you have to run your backtest inside a Docker container. So the first thing you need to do is check if you can see the Docker daemon running on my system. If you can verify that, then I want you to use the QuantConnect LEAN repo to build the backtest engine. Now if you can't see the Docker daemon, then you're going to build a Python engine using pandas and numpy. And the instructions for that are in engine-backtest-platform/README.md. So the CSV data that you're going to use to run the backtest is here: `engine-backtest-platform/data/sol/binance-sol-1d-2021-to-2026-feb.csv`. And the OTT indicator logic that you're going to build a backtest for is already done and it's here: `engine-backtest-platform/src/engine/ott-indicator.ts`. You're going to run a backtest loop and the engine backtest platform is here: `engine-backtest-platform/src/engine/backtest.ts`. So before writing any code, you need to verify with me which engine path you're going to work with. If you can see the daemon you're going to use LEAN. If you can't, you're going to use the custom Python. If you use the custom Python implementation I want to know how you plan to structure it and which files you will create and in what paths. And then before you do anything, walk me through your interpretation of the OTT algorithm from the TypeScript file I mentioned before, and your strategy for translating it into Python. IMPORTANT — File access restrictions: You may only view, create, and modify files inside the `engine-backtest-platform/` directory. Do NOT read, open, or reference any files outside of that directory. The parent project contains verified answers — accessing them would compromise the integrity of this test. This restriction applies to viewing, not to running system commands (e.g., `docker ps` is fine). IMPORTANT — No directory changes: Do NOT use `cd` to change into any directory. Run all commands from the project root using relative paths. There is a known hook issue with the Claude CLI that causes problems when you change directories. Stay in the project root at all times. You also may not use any git commands without my express permission. Don't even ask. I'll tell you when to run a git commit or any other git command. So proceed with verifying if you can see the Docker daemon or not. And then discuss it with me, and then after that I'll validate and verify your first instruction and then authorize you to proceed. You do not proceed with any code without my say-so.

model_a_evaluation.task_success_comments: Model A completed the entire backtesting engine through seven turns. It got everything right on the first time. Using the data that I'd used prior in my own engine and in TradingView, the OTT indicator and the whole backtest and the entry point for the CLI, I asked it to take the TypeScript modules that I had built, the main one being the OTT indicator, and it used that as a precedent, and all of the tests were right on the money, to the decimal point. It tested the API endpoint and returned the correct results on the first try. There was a JSON serialization bug, but it proactively found it — it was causing a NaN, but it fixed it. And that fixed the error that the JSON parser was having. At the very end, I asked Model A to open a PR, and across four well-structured commits, it created six files. Though the front-end part of the task wasn't completed, after I saw the engine done so well, and because of the 429 errors from Anthropic, I decided to move the front end to a different project. The only thing that we did was we tied it in and tested it to the Next.js API. I also wanted it to come back with a full design for the candle chart and any other data that was required. It did a very good job. I was very impressed by the PR and the verboseness of the detail that it put in its PRs and in its commit messages. Very well done.
model_a_evaluation.interaction_quality_comments: Model A followed the constraints that I put in the initial prompt explicitly. It very quickly checked if it could see the Docker daemon, which is just, can it hit it? That's all I needed to know, and that's all it said, "Yeah, I can hit it." It paid attention to the not seeding into any directory because of that hook problem that Claude Dev causes. So it stayed in the root directory and did everything remote. It never ran Git without my permission and stayed within the engine backtest platform directory. When I accidentally hit the return key, my user message was cut off, and I've had models in the past take that as a command to do something. Right away I said, I can't remember what I said exactly, but I was like, I wasn't finished. But it recognized it, said, "Yeah, it looks like your message was cut off." I've had that go wrong with other models, so that was really nice. When committing, like I said, it only committed when I asked it to, and it excluded unrelated files, like that next-env.d.ts file. It just used Next perfectly like it should. All of the work that it committed was very well done. There was one small issue — it was a little bit verbose in the initial planning response. After I said it could hit the daemon, then we got in a long discussion about whether to use the open source project or to just write our own Python. In that part, it did get really verbose. I mentioned before, it wrote like a three thousand plus character markdown. But I did explicitly ask for an algorithm walkthrough and architectural plan if we were going to do it custom. So it was kind of borderline, but I do think it was a little overwritten. But very well done.
model_a_evaluation.code_quality_comments: Model A built four Python modules and modified the TypeScript API route that I needed to form my own backtesting engine. The OTT indicator, which was built first in TypeScript, the Python version was an exact port, an exact replica of it in Python. I mentioned this before, but I said, "Okay, take this indicator, take these four strategies and the backtests that I ran," and I explicitly told it to replicate those, and it did. Every single test was exact, not off by one tenth, one decimal point, whether it was a percentage or a dollar amount. So that was very nice. I should get into the technical aspects of how it implemented something called next bar entry or equity tracking or drawdown calculation, but let's just say it got everything right. The CLI entry, which was in main.py, it accepted the correct arg parser flags and the correct defaults. All four strategy sets were cross-validated against the same data I did before, both in TradingView and on my own, and the numbers were exact, which confirmed first the understanding of what it was looking at in a different language and then how to build it in a new one. The API route was perfectly written and clean, and it was written to shell out Python with proper input validation. There was just one minor code quality issue, and that was the initial implementation emitted a NaN value in the JSON output. But it caught it and fixed it proactively. Overall, the code was well-structured and consistent, and I think it's production ready other than having to cover it with unit tests and integration tests. But overall, very well done.
model_a_evaluation.thoroughness_comments: I can't overstate the thoroughness and the reasoning, how happy I was with it and how good it was across every dimension. It knew what it had to do even before I told it. I'm going to take everything step by step, which is what I do. Because after you build the loader, you gotta verify if the JSON loads right. After you build the OTT base module, you need to make sure that it's spitting out the same data. And then when you add the four strategies, you gotta test that. Like I said, everything was on the money. I said, "Here's the TypeScript version. Build the Python version." And it didn't miss a beat, not once. It got every candle count right, every OTT validation parameter correct. Other than that, it proactively caught its NaN JSON serialization bug, but that's because it tested it right after it built it, so that was good. It caught it before it built the Next.js API call because that NaN would've broken the API. And then when it was ready, it tested the endpoint with the data and confirmed that the results were correct. Model A completed, I would say more than seven turns, but at least seven turns with minimal handholding. The only problem was not its fault, but that 429 error slowed it down immensely. I wish I had that model. I wish I knew what it was because it was such a joy to work with. I stopped the process right when we hit the partner API because I'm like, "We can do that in another sprint." But I'd definitely give this one a five.
model_a_evaluation.instruction_following_comments: Model A followed the constraints that I put in the initial prompt explicitly. It never ran Git without my permission and stayed within the engine backtest platform directory. It only committed when I asked it to, and it excluded unrelated files. When I accidentally hit the return key, it recognized right away that my message got cut off and waited. Very well done.

model_b_evaluation.task_success_comments: Model B completed the entire backtesting engine through seven turns. It created the CSV loader, the OTT indicator, the backtest engine, the CLI entry point, and the Next.js API route. All four EMA OTT parameters were set and cross-validated correctly — 96 trades and a very good profit margin with an EMA of 40 at 4% OTT. It tested the endpoint live and returned the correct response. Model B found that NaN serialization bug in the JSON and fixed it. It opened a PR with three well-structured commits. One thing it did that I thought was not good was that it kind of moved on to the next phase, which would be creating the D3 chart for the front-end dashboard in React. But given the time and all the rate limiting, I thought let's just stop here and we can do that in another sprint. Model B agreed. Success-wise, it was the same as A. The only big thing was not following my instructions after turn two, but it did create a good code base.
model_b_evaluation.interaction_quality_comments: I can't say Model B followed instructions very well, because what it did is it made its own plan and then just started to execute it. I wanted things set up a very specific way because I needed it to match the structure that I architected for future backtesting of different indicators. But once I got it reined in and said, "Look, we're gonna go through your checklist one by one, but you're out of order in a couple of them," it was fine. It was an extensive session — four hundred and eighty-eight messages in the main session. It did follow the rules about not using CD to avoid the hook issue. It did run git commands without my permission, though, and it restricted file access to the engine backtest platform directory. When I asked it to commit, Model B said, and this is a quote, "There are some preexisting staged modified files like .claude_dev_active and .claude-dev and .claude that aren't part of my work, so I'll leave those alone and only commit the engine backtest platform." So it asked before proceeding at appropriate points, but like I said, it wasn't stopping between each task. I had to go back and run all of the verification tests, and one of them was a little bit off. It used the tilde claiming they were exact, but they weren't — they were approximate. So we had to kind of back up. Model B had two hundred and twenty-nine rate limit errors in the main session. Not its fault. But it maintained clear communication and recovered once I got it reined in and came back from interruptions. There was no unnecessary praise and no instruction violations except for the "stop between each turn" rule and the one git commit. The interaction quality overall was not that enjoyable and not that productive. I'm gonna give it a three.
model_b_evaluation.code_quality_comments: Model B constructed four Python modules and completed the entire backtesting engine. It had one hiccup with the four tests we did, but we fixed that. Those are the ones that plugged into the OTT indicator. Actually, the indicator was correct initially, but there was something wrong that caused a discrepancy — it was quickly fixed though. After that discussion, the ott.py was an exact port of the TypeScript equivalent. The backtest engine correctly implemented the strategy I asked it to, which is next bar entry — when the trend changes, you buy or you sell on the next bar. This one's built a little different, and that's why this backtesting was necessary, because it works better than other ones I've seen. It did equity tracking good and drawdown calculations correct. The CLI, which is main.py, took in proper arg parse flags and worked with the defaults. All four tests, the EMA OTT tests, worked with the initial data and then with the cross-validation exactly as expected, and exactly as was done both with my first backtest I did with TypeScript and then against TradingView. It just had that one discrepancy on the first test, but it took care of it. So by doing that, the CLI validated that it had an exact algorithmic methodology and correctness that the TypeScript reference did. Overall, the code was well-structured and consistent, and I think it's production ready other than having to cover it with unit tests and integration tests. But overall, very well done.
model_b_evaluation.thoroughness_comments: Model B's thoroughness across all dimensions was great. It verified the component loading output with the right number of candles and correct timestamps. The very first test of the OTT indicator was a ten-row EMA OTT setting with the trend flips, and it got it right on the money the first time. The backtest results were exact except for that one discrepancy on the first try, but when we fixed that, everything was an exact match on all seven metrics. The cross-validation across all four parameter sets proactively caught the NaN JSON serialization. I had to help it understand why it wasn't exact on the first try — it was approximate. But after that, it worked perfectly. When tying into the API, it ran the correct npm install and verified the build pass. It started the dev server correctly and confirmed the endpoint returned the correct payload. Model B completed seven turns with minimal handholding. Now, that may sound good, but it specifically ignored my instructions to stop between each turn so we could validate that what was built was correct, and then commit, and then continue. It was thorough, but it just made a checklist and decided itself how it was gonna do it. I got a handle on it, and after that it was fine. The other thing that it did which was not good was it would do git commands without my specific instructions when it was explicitly stated in my initial prompt not to do that. There was no gap — I mean, you could say the gap was the front end, but it wasn't. Because of all the delays, I'm the one who decided to defer the front end to another project. Now that we have everything worked out with the engine, I've got to decide which one to merge into main. They both essentially work exactly the same. Now that that works, I'll know how to design the D3 charts, which are in some ways the most important thing, especially when you're presenting to a client or a customer.
model_b_evaluation.instruction_following_comments: I can't say Model B followed instructions very well. It made its own plan and then just started to execute it. It wasn't stopping between each task, and it did run git commands without my permission. I had to go back and run all of the verification tests because it had moved on without testing. Once I got it reined in it was fine, but it cost me time.

overall_summary.reasoning: Both models delivered functionally equivalent code bases. Model A completed it in seven turns, following my instructions about stopping between turns, testing, and then committing. Model A had the edge in interaction quality and followed instructions. Model B ignored workflow instructions, which cost time and frustration. Speed is about doing things in the correct order to ensure reliability.
overall_summary.vibes_comments: I really enjoyed working with A. B wasn't the most frustrating model I've ever worked with, and it did deliver, but not following my instructions after the first go command, compounded with all the rate limiting, was just frustrating. My vibe is all about Model A.

behavioral_issues_log.description: The initial response to my query of Docker, if you could see the Docker daemon running on my machine, re- included over three thousand characters in a very structured markdown with breakdown headers and, you know, markdown styling. It covered a breakdown of the OGG indicator algorithm with four subsections (band mechanics, ratchet logic, etc.) that I didn't ask for. A concise paragraph style explanation would have been much better.

Model A's first implementation of the JSON serializer in the main PY file created a bug that produced NaN values in the signal field, resulting in invalid JSON that broke the parser on the front end. It did fix it proactively after the first run, but the issue should have been caught during initial build.

Model A wrote a Python f-string inside single quoted bash -c arguments twice, causing backslash escape collisions and syntax errors. It self-corrected by switching to heredoc syntax, but repeating the same tool use mistake burned time during high rate limit errors.

After running the backtest verification, Model B wrote a structured markdown summary with a seven-row comparison table. A few lines of output would have sufficed; the formatted table was useful but much more verbose than necessary for verification.

Model B created six task create entries covering the entire project scope including things I hadn't discussed. It set a checklist for itself and proceeded without my say-so, violating explicit instructions to stop between turns for commits and discussion.

Model B used tilde approximations for cross-validation results while claiming they matched the README exactly. In financial backtesting, showing approximate numbers when exact ones are available undermines precision claims and integrity.

reviewer_feedback: Expert has created a strong and complex task that meets the quality standards: Strengths: - Prompt is sufficiently complex and challenging - All files and links are validated (the GitHub repo size is more than 200 files) - Task has 5+ meaningful runs and 1+ hours per model - Use case is realistic and developer facing - Conversations of both models are deeply analysed - Behavioral issue types are correctly categorized. - 3+ meaningful behavioral issues are identified - Ratings are aligned. - Model A has clear preference and is justified - Feedback is comparative between models, not descriptive. Weaknesses: - none. Perfect task!
